subject: Physics
q: A plane electromagnetic wave is moving in free space with velocity $c=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and its electric field is given as $\vec{E}=54 \sin (k z-\omega t) \hat{j} \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$, where $\hat{j}$ is the unit vector along $y$-axis. The magnetic field vector $\vec{B}$ of the wave is:
question: A plane electromagnetic wave is moving in free space with velocity $c=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and its electric field is given as $\vec{E}=54 \sin (k z-\omega t) \hat{j} \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$, where $\hat{j}$ is the unit vector along $y$-axis. The magnetic field vector $\vec{B}$ of the wave is:
solution:
For an electromagnetic wave, the relationship between electric and magnetic field amplitudes is $B_0 = \frac{E_0}{c}$.
Here $E_0 = 54$ V/m and $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
So, $B_0 = \frac{54}{3 \times 10^8} = 1.8 \times 10^{-7}$ T.
The electric field is along the $\hat{j}$ direction.
Since the wave travels in the $+z$ direction, the magnetic field direction is determined by $\vec{E} \times \vec{B}$ pointing in the $+z$ direction.
Using the right-hand rule: $\hat{j} \times (-\hat{i}) = \hat{k}$ (positive $z$ direction), confirming the magnetic field is in the $-\hat{i}$ direction.
Therefore, $\vec{B} = -1.8 \times 10^{-7}\sin(kz - \omega t)\hat{i}$ T.

subject: Physics
q: The energy density associated with electric field E→ and magnetic field B→ of an electromagnetic wave in free space is given by (ε0- permittivity of free space, μ0- permeability of free space)
question: The energy density associated with electric field E→ and magnetic field B→ of an electromagnetic wave in free space is given by (ε0- permittivity of free space, μ0- permeability of free space)
solution:
When an electromagnetic wave propagates from the source, it transfers energy to the objects in its path. Electric and magnetic fields serve as energy reservoirs for electromagnetic waves. An electromagnetic wave contains the same amount of energy as are contained in the electric and magnetic fields together. In such situation, the energy density of the electromagnetic wave is equal to the sum of the energies of the electric and magnetic fields.

The average electric and magnetic energy densities are given by

UE=12ε0E2UB=12B2μ0

subject: Physics
q: An electromagnetic wave with frequency $\omega$ and wavelength $\lambda$ travels in the $+y$ direction. Its magnetic field is along $+x$-axis. The vector equation for the associated electric field (of amplitude $E_0$ ) is
question: An electromagnetic wave with frequency $\omega$ and wavelength $\lambda$ travels in the $+y$ direction. Its magnetic field is along $+x$-axis. The vector equation for the associated electric field (of amplitude $E_0$ ) is
solution:
In an electromagnetic wave electric field and magnetic field are perpendicular to the direction of propagation of wave. The vector equation for the electric field is
$$
\vec{E}=E_0 \cos \left(\omega t-\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} y\right) \hat{z}
$$

subject: Physics
q: Which is the correct ascending order of wavelengths?
question: Which is the correct ascending order of wavelengths?
solution:
We know that E=hcλ⇒E∝1λ. Also,

Egamma-ray>EX-ray>Evisible>Emicrowave

Therefore,

λgamma-ray<λX-ray<λvisible<λmicrowave

subject: Physics
q:
A laser beam has intensity of $4.0 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$. The amplitude of magnetic field associated with beam is $\_\_\_\_$ T.
(Take $\epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^{2} / \mathrm{Nm}^{2}$ and $\mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$)
question:
A laser beam has intensity of $4.0 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$. The amplitude of magnetic field associated with beam is $\_\_\_\_$ T.
(Take $\epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^{2} / \mathrm{Nm}^{2}$ and $\mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$)
solution:
The intensity of an electromagnetic wave is
$I = \frac{1}{2} \epsilon_0 c E_0^2$.
From the relation $E_0 = c B_0$, we get $I = \frac{1}{2} \epsilon_0 c^3 B_0^2$.
Solving for magnetic field amplitude:
$B_0 = \sqrt{\frac{2I}{\epsilon_0 c^3}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 4.0 \times 10^{14}}{8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times (3 \times 10^8)^3}} = \sqrt{\frac{8.0 \times 10^{14}}{2.39 \times 10^{14}}}$
$= \sqrt{3.347} = 1.83$ T.

solution:
E→=E0 i^ cos kz cosωt

B→=E02C j^ cos kz-ωt+E02Cj^ coskz+ωt

subject: Physics
q: An alternating voltage of amplitude $40 \mathrm{~V}$ and frequency $4 \mathrm{kHz}$ is applied directly across the capacitor of $12 \mu \mathrm{F}$. The maximum displacement current between the plates of the capacitor is nearly :
question: An alternating voltage of amplitude $40 \mathrm{~V}$ and frequency $4 \mathrm{kHz}$ is applied directly across the capacitor of $12 \mu \mathrm{F}$. The maximum displacement current between the plates of the capacitor is nearly :
solution:
Displacement current is same as conduction current in capacitor.
$\begin{aligned}
& X_C=\frac{1}{\omega \mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{2 \pi \mathrm{fC}} \\
& =\frac{1}{2 \pi \times 4 \times 10^3 \times 12 \times 10^{-6}}=3.317 \Omega
\end{aligned}$
$I=\frac{V}{X_C}=\frac{40}{3.317}=12 \mathrm{~A}$

Considering a case of complete absorption, the energy flux of incident light is
question: Sun light falls normally on a surface of area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and exerts an average force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> within a time period of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> minutes. Considering a case of complete absorption, the energy flux of incident light is
solution:
The radiation pressure in case of complete absorption is given by,

P=Ic.

Therefore, the force will be,

F=Ic×A⇒I=FcA

⇒I=7.2×10-9×3×10836×10-4=600 W m-2⇒I=0.06 W cm-2

subject: Physics
q:
The ratio of speeds of electromagnetic waves in vacuum and a medium, having dielectric constant $k=3$ and permeability of $\mu=2 \mu_{0}$, is
($\mu_{0}=$ permeability of vacuum)
question:
The ratio of speeds of electromagnetic waves in vacuum and a medium, having dielectric constant $k=3$ and permeability of $\mu=2 \mu_{0}$, is
($\mu_{0}=$ permeability of vacuum)
solution:
Speed of electromagnetic wave in vacuum: $c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \epsilon_0}}$
Speed in a medium with permeability $\mu = 2\mu_0$ and dielectric constant $k = 3$:
$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu \epsilon}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\mu_0 \times 3\epsilon_0}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6\mu_0\epsilon_0}}$
$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0\epsilon_0}} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{6}}$
Ratio of speeds: $\frac{c}{v} = \sqrt{6}$
Therefore: $c : v = \sqrt{6} : 1$

subject: Physics
q:
The intensity of the light from a bulb incident on a surface is 0.22 W m-2. The amplitude of the magnetic field in this light-wave is _____ ×10-9 T

(Given : Permittivity of vacuum ϵ0=8.85×10-12 C2 N-1 m-2, speed of light in vacuum c=3×108 m s-1)
question:
The intensity of the light from a bulb incident on a surface is 0.22 W m-2. The amplitude of the magnetic field in this light-wave is _____ ×10-9 T

(Given : Permittivity of vacuum ϵ0=8.85×10-12 C2 N-1 m-2, speed of light in vacuum c=3×108 m s-1)
solution:
The intensity is defined as energy per unit time per unit area. Therefore, I=dEAdt.

Energy density is energy per unit volume. Therefore, Ud=dEAdx.

Dividing IUd=dxdt=c

⇒Ud=Ic=0.223×108=223×10-10 J m-3

Magnetic energy density will be half of total energy density, Therefore, Ub=113×10-10 J m-3.

Now energy density in magnetic field is given by, Ub=B22μ0. Therefore,

Brms22μ0=113×10-10 J m-3 where Brms2=B02=B022

⇒B024μ0=113×10-10⇒B0=4×4π×10-7×113×10-10⇒B0=4.29×10-8 T≃43×10-9 T

question: The magnetic field of a plane electromagnetic wave is given by <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ω</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ω</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, where <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>RMS</mi></math> value of the force experienced by a stationary charge <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> at <math><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is closest to
solution:
B→=B0i^coskz-ωt+B1j^coskz+ωt
B0=3×10-5 T &  B1=2×10-6 T
Electric filed associated with it is,
E→=-B0cj^coskz-ωt-B1ci^coskz+ωt
Here, c is the speed of light in vacuum.
At z=0,
Frms=QB0c2+QB1c22
Here c is speed of light in vacuum.
=10-4×3×10-5×3×1082+10-4×2×10-6×3×10822
=0.81+0.00362=0.6 N

\hline
\end{array}$
solution: Infrared is the least energetic thus having biggest wavelength $(\lambda) \&$ gamma rays are most energetic thus having smallest wavelength $(\lambda)$.

subject: Physics
q: A monochromatic source of light operating at $15$ kW emits $2.5\times 10^{22}$ photons/s. The region of an electromagnetic spectrum to which the emitted electromagnetic radiation belongs to ________. (Take $h=6.6\times 10^{-34}$ J·s and $c=3\times 10^8$ m/s).
question: A monochromatic source of light operating at $15$ kW emits $2.5\times 10^{22}$ photons/s. The region of an electromagnetic spectrum to which the emitted electromagnetic radiation belongs to ________. (Take $h=6.6\times 10^{-34}$ J·s and $c=3\times 10^8$ m/s).
solution:
Power of the source, $P = 15 \text{ kW} = 15 \times 10^3 \text{ J/s}$

Number of photons emitted per second, $n = 2.5 \times 10^{22} \text{ s}^{-1}$

Energy of one photon, $E = \dfrac{P}{n} = \dfrac{15 \times 10^3}{2.5 \times 10^{22}} = 6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

Using $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$, we get $\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{E}$

$\lambda = \dfrac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^{-19}} = 3.3 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 330 \text{ nm}$

The wavelength $330 \text{ nm}$ lies in the ultraviolet region of the electromagnetic spectrum.

Answer: Ultraviolet

subject: Physics
q: A point light source emits E.M. waves in free space. A detector, placed at a distance of $L$ m, measures the intensity as $I_o$. The detector is now shifted to another location on the same spherical surface ensuring the angle between original location and new location as $45°$. The measured intensity at new location will be _______.
question: A point light source emits E.M. waves in free space. A detector, placed at a distance of $L$ m, measures the intensity as $I_o$. The detector is now shifted to another location on the same spherical surface ensuring the angle between original location and new location as $45°$. The measured intensity at new location will be _______.
solution:
For a point light source, the intensity of electromagnetic waves at a distance $r$ is given by $I = \dfrac{P}{4\pi r^2}$, where $P$ is the power of the source.

Since the detector is moved to a new location on the same spherical surface, the distance from the source remains $L$.

The emission from a point source is isotropic, meaning the intensity depends only on the radial distance from the source and is independent of the angular position.

Therefore, the intensity at the new location remains unchanged, which is $I_o$.

Answer: $I_o$

subject: Physics
q: If frequency of electromagnetic wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math> and it travels in air along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> direction then the corresponding electric and magnetic field vectors will be mutually perpendicular to each other and the wavelength of the wave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>in</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is :
question: If frequency of electromagnetic wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math> and it travels in air along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> direction then the corresponding electric and magnetic field vectors will be mutually perpendicular to each other and the wavelength of the wave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>in</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is :
solution:
As speed of electromagnetic wave is given by, c=fλ. Therefore, 

λ=cf=3×10860×106=5 m.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : EM waves used for optical communication have longer wavelengths than that of microwave, employed in Radar technology.

Reason R : Infrared EM waves are more energetic than microwaves, (used in Radar)

In the light of given statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : EM waves used for optical communication have longer wavelengths than that of microwave, employed in Radar technology.

Reason R : Infrared EM waves are more energetic than microwaves, (used in Radar)

In the light of given statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is false but R is true
A is true but R is false
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
solution:
An optical telecommunication is communication at a distance using light to carry information. It can be performed visually or by using electronic devices.

The wavelength of the microwaves are greater than that of the common optical light waves.

Hence, the Assertion is not correct. But, because of the small wavelength, the electromagnetic waves are more energetic than the microwaves. Hence, the reason is true.

q: The frequency of $X$-rays; $\gamma$-rays and ultraviolet rays are respectively $a, b$ and $c$ then
question: The frequency of $X$-rays; $\gamma$-rays and ultraviolet rays are respectively $a, b$ and $c$ then
solution:
Frequency range of $\gamma$-ray, $b=10^{18}-10^{23} \mathrm{~Hz}$
Frequency range of X-ray, $a=10^{16}-10^{20} \mathrm{~Hz}$
Frequency range of ultraviolet ray, $c=10^{15}-10^{17} \mathrm{~Hz}$
$\therefore a < b ; b>c$

subject: Physics
q: An electromagnetic wave travelling in $x$-direction is described by field equation $E_y = 300\sin\omega\left(t - \dfrac{x}{c}\right)$. If the electron is restricted to move in $y$-direction only with speed of $1.5 \times 10^6$ m/s then ratio of maximum electric and magnetic forces acting on the electron is _______.
question: An electromagnetic wave travelling in $x$-direction is described by field equation $E_y = 300\sin\omega\left(t - \dfrac{x}{c}\right)$. If the electron is restricted to move in $y$-direction only with speed of $1.5 \times 10^6$ m/s then ratio of maximum electric and magnetic forces acting on the electron is _______.
solution:
The maximum electric force acting on the electron is $F_e = eE_0$.

The maximum magnetic force acting on the electron is $F_m = evB_0$, where $v$ is the speed of the electron and $B_0$ is the maximum magnetic field.

The ratio of the maximum electric force to the maximum magnetic force is given by $\dfrac{F_e}{F_m} = \dfrac{eE_0}{evB_0} = \dfrac{E_0}{vB_0}$.

For an electromagnetic wave, the ratio of the amplitudes of the electric and magnetic fields is $\dfrac{E_0}{B_0} = c$, where $c$ is the speed of light in vacuum.

Substituting this into the ratio of forces yields $\dfrac{F_e}{F_m} = \dfrac{c}{v}$.

Given $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s and $v = 1.5 \times 10^6$ m/s, we get $\dfrac{F_e}{F_m} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 200$.

Answer: $200$

subject: Physics
q: The electric field of a plane electromagnetic wave, travelling in an unknown nonmagnetic medium is given by, $E_{\mathrm{y}}=20 \sin \left(3 \times 10^{6} x-4.5 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{t}\right) \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{m}$ (where $x, \mathrm{t}$ and other values have S.I. units). The dielectric constant of the medium is $\_\_\_\_$ (speed of light in free space is $3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$)
question: The electric field of a plane electromagnetic wave, travelling in an unknown nonmagnetic medium is given by, $E_{\mathrm{y}}=20 \sin \left(3 \times 10^{6} x-4.5 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{t}\right) \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{m}$ (where $x, \mathrm{t}$ and other values have S.I. units). The dielectric constant of the medium is $\_\_\_\_$ (speed of light in free space is $3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$)
solution:
From the wave equation $E_y = 20\sin(3 \times 10^6 x - 4.5 \times 10^{14} t)$:
Wave number: $k = 3 \times 10^6$ m⁻¹
Angular frequency: $\omega = 4.5 \times 10^{14}$ rad/s
Speed of wave in medium: $v = \frac{\omega}{k} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{14}}{3 \times 10^6} = 1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s
Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
Refractive index: $n = \frac{c}{v} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5 \times 10^8} = 2$
For a nonmagnetic medium: $n = \sqrt{\epsilon_r}$
Dielectric constant: $\epsilon_r = n^2 = 4$

subject: Physics
q:
Match List - I with List - II.
$\begin{array}{l} \text{List - I} & \text{List - II} \\ \text{Relation} & \text{Law} \\ \text{A. } \oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=-\frac{d}{d t} \oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d a} & \text{I. Ampere's circuital law} \\ \text{B. } \oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0}\left(I+\epsilon_{0} \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}\right) & \text{II. Faraday's laws of} \\ & \text{electromagnetic induction} \\ \text{C. } \oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d a}=\frac{1}{\epsilon_{0}} \int_{\mathrm{V}} \rho \mathrm{dv} & \text{III. Ampere - Maxwell law} \\ \text{D. } \oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I & \text{IV. Gauss's law of electrostatics} \end{array}$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Match List - I with List - II.
$\begin{array}{l} \text{List - I} & \text{List - II} \\ \text{Relation} & \text{Law} \\ \text{A. } \oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=-\frac{d}{d t} \oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d a} & \text{I. Ampere's circuital law} \\ \text{B. } \oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0}\left(I+\epsilon_{0} \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}\right) & \text{II. Faraday's laws of} \\ & \text{electromagnetic induction} \\ \text{C. } \oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d a}=\frac{1}{\epsilon_{0}} \int_{\mathrm{V}} \rho \mathrm{dv} & \text{III. Ampere - Maxwell law} \\ \text{D. } \oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=\mu_{0} I & \text{IV. Gauss's law of electrostatics} \end{array}$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Theoretical
A-II, B-III, C-IV, D-I

A. $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -\frac{d}{dt} \oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{a}$ → Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction (II)
B. $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 \left(I + \epsilon_0 \frac{d\Phi_E}{dt}\right)$ → Ampere-Maxwell law (III)
C. $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{a} = \frac{1}{\epsilon_0} \int \rho \, dv$ → Gauss's law of electrostatics (IV)
D. $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I$ → Ampere's circuital law (I)

subject: Physics
q:
Match List I (Wavelength range of electromagnetic spectrum) with List II (Method of production of these waves) and select the correct option from the options given below the lists.
  List I   List II
(a) 700 nm to 1 mm (i) Vibration of atoms and molecules.
(b) 1 nm to 400 nm (ii) Inner shell electrons in atoms moving from one energy level to a lower level.
(c) < 10-3 nm (iii) Radioactive decay of the nucleus.
(d) 1 mm to 0.1 m (iv) Magnetron valve.
question:
Match List I (Wavelength range of electromagnetic spectrum) with List II (Method of production of these waves) and select the correct option from the options given below the lists.
  List I   List II
(a) 700 nm to 1 mm (i) Vibration of atoms and molecules.
(b) 1 nm to 400 nm (ii) Inner shell electrons in atoms moving from one energy level to a lower level.
(c) < 10-3 nm (iii) Radioactive decay of the nucleus.
(d) 1 mm to 0.1 m (iv) Magnetron valve.
solution:
We know, the range of wavelengths between 700 nm to 1 mm, is referred as Infrared rays. These are produced by heating an object to such an extent that the molecules start vibrating and it radiates infrared waves. When the object is not hot enough such that it can emit visible light, therefore, it radiates Infrared waves.
Thus, option (a) in list I matches with Option (i) in list II.


The range of wavelengths between 1 nm to 400 nm, is referred to as ultraviolet rays. Ultraviolet rays are produced due to atomic excitation in a gaseous discharge tube. This atomic excitation occurs when an atom from one energy level jumps to a lower energy level.
Thus, option (b) in list I matches with Option (ii) in list II.

The range of wavelengths <10-3 nm are known as the Gamma rays. Some of the radioactive substances, when decay releases a spectrum, which falls in the range of <10-3 nm, they emit electromagnetic waves called the gamma rays.
Thus, we can say, Thus, option (c) in list I matches with Option (iii) in list II.

We know again, the range of wavelength 1 mm to 0.1 m, is referred to as microwaves, and they are produced inside an oven in a tube like structure, called the magnetron.

Thus, we can say, option (d) in list I matches with Option (iv) in list II.

solution:
E0=800 V

B0=E0c=8003×108

Fm=qvB0=1.6×10-19×3×107×8003×108

=12.8×10-18 N

q:

B→x, t=1.2×10−7sin0.5×103x+1.5×1011tk^ T.

The instantaneous electric field E→ corresponding to B→ is :
question:
For a plane electromagnetic wave, the magnetic field at a point x and time t is :

B→x, t=1.2×10−7sin0.5×103x+1.5×1011tk^ T.

The instantaneous electric field E→ corresponding to B→ is :
solution:
E0=B0×C=1.2×10-7×3×108=36

As the light is propagating in x direction.

& E^×B^||C^

∴ E→ should be in j^ direction.

So electric field E→=E0sinE→·(x,t)=-36sin0.5×103x+1.5×1011tj^Vm
